segments.s080-g97: تو گفتی که introduces a reported clause: تو (you) + گفتی (you said) and که (that) marks the subordinate content. سام is the subject of the subordinate clause. یلستی is a predicative adjective/descriptor attached after the noun (an archaic/poetic form meaning “manly/heroic” or “possessing heroism”). به جای begins a prepositional phrase (“with respect to / in the matter of place/position”) that will be continued by a series of nouns in the next segment.
segments.s080-g98: This continues the prepositional phrase begun by به جای: a single preposition به governs a coordinated list of nouns joined by و. بالا here means an exalted or high status; دیدار is used poetically for public regard/renown (though literally 'meeting/sight'); فرهنگ denotes learning or cultivated excellence; رای means counsel/judgment. The conjunctions و simply join the items enumerated under the same prepositional scope.
segments.s080-g9: شگفت اندرو مانده بد مرد و زن is a verbless/stative clause in which شگفت (“wonder/astonishment”) is the state that “remained” (مانده) “in/at this” (اندرو). مانده is a past participial/preterite form meaning “remained” and takes the people (مرد و زن) as its experiencers; the adverbial بد intensifies the state (here roughly “greatly/very”). Word order is topical (state + locative/target + predicate + experiencers).
segments.s080-g10: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “that,” and the verb نشنید is a past negative “(had) not heard/of” (poetic order places verb before کس). کس here is negative/indefinite “anyone/no one.” بچه پیل تن is a nominal phrase in which بچه = “child” is qualified by پیل تن (“elephant-bodied” / “of the body of an elephant”), a compressed genitive/attributive description.
segments.s080-g11: همان is a demonstrative/adverbial “that very/that same,” modifying دردگاهش (“his/its sore spot” — دردگاه + pronominal suffix -ش). فرو is an adverbial prefix meaning “inward/down/deeply” and دوختند is the past plural verb “they sewed/stitched.” The verb is final (classical order) and the agent “they” is implicit.
segments.s080-g12: به دارو is a dative/instrumental prepositional phrase “to/into the remedy/with the medicine,” followed by the patient/theme همه درد “all the pain.” بسپوختند is a classical perfect/past plural (with prefix ب‑) meaning “they entrusted/surrendered” (i.e. they committed the pains to the medicine). The sentence has an omitted subject (they) recovered from context.
segments.s080-g13: شبانروز is an adverbial “day and night / constantly” modifying the clause. مادر is the subject and ز + می‌خفته بود literally gives the cause/state “was from wine‑sleep” — می‌خفته is a classical past participial/continuous meaning ‘was asleep/drunk’ and بود is the past auxiliary “was.” The order places the adverbial first for emphasis.
segments.s080-g14: The clause repeats ز می‌خفته (“from that wine‑sleep”) and then uses the interjection هش (“alas!”) followed by ازو رفته بود “had gone from her.” ازو is the 3rd‑person oblique pronoun “from her,” رفته بود is the pluperfect “had gone,” so the whole phrase says her senses/consciousness had departed. The interjection precedes the verbal clause for poetic effect.
segments.s080-g15: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” از خواب بیدار شد is the verbal phrase “woke up / became awake” (بیدار + شد, with شد as past auxiliary/verb). سرو بن is the person who performs the action (inverted after the verb for metrical/poetic order); here سرو بن functions as a descriptive name/epithet (literally ‘cypress‑root’/a tall youth).
segments.s080-g16: به سیندخت is a prepositional phrase marking the addressee “to Sindukht.” بگشاد is a past form from گشودن “opened” (here reflexive of opening the mouth), and لب بر سخن is a phraseal idiom “his lip upon speech” = “he opened his lips to speak.” Word order places the verb before the object phrase for poetic rhythm.
segments.s080-g41: Word order: the indefinite numeral/word 'یکی' (= 'one' / 'a') precedes the object 'جشن' and the verb 'کردند' (3rd person plural past of کردن) which here means 'held/made' a celebration. The locative prepositional phrase 'در گلستان' follows the verb and specifies where the celebration took place.
segments.s080-g42: 'ز ... تا ... به ...' marks a geographic span: 'ز' is the poetic 'from', 'تا' gives the extent 'as far as', and 'به' introduces the endpoint 'Kabulestan'. The whole chunk means 'from Zavolestan to Kabulestan'.
segments.s080-g43: 'همه دشت' = 'the whole/plain entirety' is the topic, followed by the predicate 'پر باده و نای بود' where 'پر' is an adjective-like preposition meaning 'full (of)'. 'بود' is the past copula 'was', so the clause reads 'the whole plain was full of wine and ney (flutes)'.
segments.s080-g44: Locative phrase 'به هر کنج' ('in every corner') begins the clause; the numeral-noun 'صد مجلس' ('a hundred assemblies') is the subject phrase and 'آرای بود' (here 'آرای' meaning ornaments/arrangements and 'بود' the past copula) says they existed. Overall: 'in every corner there were a hundred festal assemblies/ornaments.'
segments.s080-g45: The phrase begins with a locative 'به زاولستان' indicating the region, then the idiom 'از کران تا کران' ('from edge to edge') indicating full extent. Together they mean 'throughout Zavolestan, from one edge to the other.'
segments.s080-g46: The past participle/adjective 'نشسته' ('seated') is used predicatively; the locative 'به هر جای' ('in every place') follows and 'رامشگران' (plural) names those seated. The clause thus states that 'comforters/entertainers were seated in every place.'
segments.s080-g47: 'نبد' is an archaic negative existential (≈ 'there was not' / 'was not'); it negates the following comparative phrase 'کهتر از مهتران' ('inferior/lesser than the elders'). 'بر فرود' indicates position 'on/at the lower place' (i.e., seated below). The whole segment says 'There was no one lesser than the elders sitting below.'
segments.s080-g48: 'نشسته' again is 'seated'; the simile 'چنان چون بود تار و پود' uses 'چنان ... چون' = 'so ... as' and 'بود' (past copula) to complete the simile: they sat 'such as (were) warp and weft' — i.e., closely interwoven. 'تار و پود' are weaving terms 'warp and weft.'
segments.s080-g1: بیامد is the narrative past 'came' and opens the clause; یکی موبدی چرب دست is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain oily‑handed mobad' where چرب دست functions as a compound adjective (literally 'oily‑handed') modifying موبدی. Word order is verb + indefinite NP (typical of older Persian narrative).
segments.s080-g2: مر is the indirect object 'for me' (preverbal here); آن ماه رخ را is a definite object 'that moon‑faced one' with را marking it as the direct object. به ... می‌کرد مست is a causative/state construction: می‌کرد (imperfective/past habitual sense 'would/made') + مست 'drunk' — together 'caused (her) to be drunk'. به here introduces the effected state ('make into/into a state of'), so the whole segment means 'he would make that moon‑faced one drunk for me.'
segments.s080-g3: بکافید here functions as an optative/imperative‑style instruction 'let it suffice / arrange (so)' directed to attendants; followed by the adverbial phrase بی‑رنج 'without trouble' and the locative phrase پهلوی ماه 'at the moon's side' (پهلوی = 'side of'). Together this asks that the placement be made easy and beside the moon‑faced one.
segments.s080-g4: بتابید is used here in the sense 'turn/bend' (an instruction to move or turn something). مر is again 'for me'; بچه را is the definite object 'the child' with را; سر ز راه is a small phrase 'head from the road' meaning 'off the road' (ز = 'from' in poetic use). So the clause issues the command 'turn the child's head away from the road for me.'
segments.s080-g5: چنان 'so/thus' modifies the following verb phrase; بی‌گزندش is a noun/adjective + enclitic pronoun meaning 'him/it unharmed' (the ش is the 3rd‑person object). برون آورید is an imperative/optative 'bring out' (آورید used as a command/request). The whole segment = 'Bring him out so unharmed.'
segments.s080-g6: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'that ...'; کس here is used in a negative context and means 'no one' (not 'someone'); در جهان = 'in the world'; این شگفتی = 'this wonder/marvel'; ندید is simple past 'did not see'. The clause says 'that no one in the world has seen this marvel.'
segments.s080-g7: یکی بچه بد is an indefinite NP 'a certain child, sickly/ill' (بد here = 'ill/poorly'). چون introduces a simile 'like'; گوی شیرفش is a poetic image 'a sphere/roundness of milk' (گوی = 'orb/ball', شیرفش a milk‑related epithet), so the whole phrase compares the child to a round, milk‑pale orb.
segments.s080-g8: به بالا بلند is a directional/complement phrase 'upwards, high' with به marking direction and بلند emphasizing height. The coordinated phrase و به دیدار کش uses کش (from کشیدن) as an imperative 'draw/bring' with به دیدار 'into sight/into view' — together: 'lift him high and bring him into sight.'
segments.s080-g33: نشاندندش is a past transitive verb with the enclitic object -ش (’him’) attached: 'they seated him'. آنگه is an adverb meaning 'then' and follows the verb here (poetic word order). بر + اسپ سمند is a prepositional phrase 'upon the steed' (بر = 'on/upon', اسپ = 'horse', سمند = specifically 'steed/warhorse').
segments.s080-g34: به گرد is a fixed locative phrase 'around' (به + گرد), اندرش = اندر + -ش is 'in/at him' → here 'around him'. چاکران is plural 'attendants/servants'. نیز چند is a short phrase 'also a few' (نیز = 'also', چند = 'several/a few'), placed after the noun as an indefinite quantifier.
segments.s080-g35: چو شد is the subordinating 'when' (چو = 'when') plus شد (here functioning as past auxiliary 'became/was'); together start a temporal clause 'when ... was'. کار یکسر همه ساخته is the clause's predicate: کار 'the affair', یکسر 'completely/entirely' (adverb), and همه ساخته uses ساخته (past participle/adj.) meaning 'all made/prepared' — literal 'the affair was entirely all made'.
segments.s080-g36: چنان چون is a doubled demonstrative/comparative 'so/as such' used for emphasis ('just as/so'), ببایست is the literary impersonal/modal form 'it was necessary/ought to', and پرداختَه is the past participle 'attended to/treated/handled'. Together they mean 'they dealt with it as was required'.
segments.s080-g37: هیون here conveys ferocity or savageness modifying تکاور (a group of elite/raiding horsemen); together they form the object phrase 'the fierce/ferocious raiders'. برانگیختند is past transitive 'they aroused/roused' (caused them to rise/stand up).
segments.s080-g38: به فرمان بران is an adverbial phrase 'by/at that command' (به فرمان = 'by command', بران = archaic/deictic 'thereon/then'). بر درم ریختند uses ریختند (past of 'to pour') with بر + درم to mean 'they poured out (dirhams)' i.e. distributed coins; بر here marks the action upon the dirhams/into coin-distribution.
segments.s080-g39: پس is the connective 'then/afterwards'. آن صورت is 'that form/likeness' (صورت = form/representation). رستم گرزدار is an appositive: 'Rostam, the mace‑bearer' (گرزدار = 'mace-holder/mace-bearing'), describing whose likeness it is.
segments.s080-g40: ببردند is a classical past-tense transitive form 'they took/carried' (a variant of بردند used in epic style). نزدیک is the prepositional/adverbial 'near', and سام سوار is 'Sam the rider' (سوار = 'rider/horseman'), so the phrase means 'they carried it near Sam the rider'.
segments.s080-g17: بر او is a prepositional phrase marking the target/recipient ('upon him'). The direct objects زر و گوهر (gold and jewel(s)) precede the past plural verb برافشاندند; برافشاندند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb (they scattered/they showered). Poetic word order places the goal phrase before the objects and the verb at the end.
segments.s080-g18: ابر کردگار is a noun phrase 'the cloud of the Creator' (ابر + modifier/possessor کردگار). آفرین is an interjection ('bravo' / 'well done') and خواندند is a past 3rd-plural verb 'they uttered/they sang/they cried out', used here to report that the cloud (poetically) voiced the exclamation.
segments.s080-g19: آن بچه را is the definite direct object 'that child' (را marks definiteness/object). پیش او is a locative phrase 'in the presence of / before him'. تاختند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb 'they rushed/ran/they hastened'. مر is an archaic enclitic/dative element (here best read as an old form meaning 'to me' / marking the speaker's perspective) placed before the object for emphasis.
segments.s080-g20: به سان is a fixed comparative phrase 'like / as'. سپهری is an adjective derived from سپهر (sky/heaven) meaning 'heavenly' or 'celestial'. برافراختند is past 3rd-plural 'they raised/exalted/erected' — together the phrase means they exalted (the child) as if celestial or raised him up like something heavenly.
segments.s080-g21: بخندید is past 3rd-person ('he/they laughed'); از can mark cause/target ('at / because of'); آن بچه را is 'that child' (را marks the object/definite); سرو سهی is an appositive/epithet describing a tall, dark cypress-like figure ('sarb / cypress, dark'), placed after the object for poetic description.
segments.s080-g22: بدید is past 'saw'. اندرو is a fused prepositional phrase 'in him / therein' (اند + رو = 'in that / in him'), linking to فر 'radiance' as the thing perceived. فر شاهنشهی is a compound meaning 'the radiance/glory of kingship' — شاهنشهی functions as a modifier 'royal/imperial', so the whole segment says '(he) saw in him the royal radiance.'
segments.s080-g23: بگفتا is past 3rd-singular 'he said'. The preceding token برستم here functions as a prepositional/name phrase ('بر رستم' = 'to/for Rostam' or 'Rostam said'), but the supplied lexical senses do not match this classical fused form (so its sense index is set to -1). غم آمد به سر is an idiom: غم آمد 'sorrow came' + به سر 'to the head' meaning 'grief seized (one)' — آمد is simple past 'came', سر is 'head'.
segments.s080-g24: نهادند is past 3rd-plural 'they set/they placed/they gave' used here in the sense 'they gave (a name)'. رستمش is رستم with the enclitic -ش (his/him) and in context functions to state the name assigned (literally 'his Rostam'); نام پسر 'the name of the son' — together 'they named the son Rostam.'
segments.s080-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'he heard'. The subject Zāl follows the verb (inverted word order common in poetry): 'When Zal heard these pithy words.' این سخنهای نغز is a noun phrase 'these pithy/special sayings' with نغز as a post‑nominal adjective.
segments.s080-g82: که introduces a relative/clausal modifier 'which/that'. روشن‌روان is a compound describing a bright or cheerful spirit (here an abstract/qualitative phrase: 'brightness of spirit' or 'cheerful feeling'). اندر آید is a verb phrase with اندر (= 'into/in') + آید (present/presentive 'comes'), a poetic present used to report what happens; به مغز = 'into the brain/mind'. Together: 'which brightened the spirit and came into the mind.'
segments.s080-g83: به شادیش is a prepositional phrase 'to/for his joy' (شاد + possessive -ش = 'his joy'). بر شادمانی uses بر as a preposition/marker meaning 'into/on to' here indicating an increase 'into rejoicing'. فزود is the past 3rd‑person singular of فزودن 'to increase' — 'it increased' or 'he increased (it)'. The clause means roughly 'his joy grew into rejoicing.'
segments.s080-g84: برافراخت is a past‑tense verb 'he raised/hoisted'. گردن is the direct object 'his neck' (object follows the verb in this poetic order). به چرخ کبود is a directional phrase: به = 'toward/against/into', چرخ = 'sky/firmament', کبود = 'azure/dark‑blue' as an adjective after the noun. Together: 'he raised his neck toward the blue sky.'
segments.s080-g85: همی is a frequentative/continuous particle often signalling ongoing or habitual action in narrative ('kept on' / 'was'). گشت here means 'went/roamed' (past narrative). چندی is a temporal adverb 'for a while'. بروبر is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'over/upon the face of' (i.e. across). جهان = 'the world'. The clause = 'he kept roaming for a while across the world.'
segments.s080-g86: برهنه شد is a two‑word predicate: برهنه (uncovered/exposed) + شد (past of شدن 'became') = 'became uncovered/revealed'. آن روزگار نهان is a noun phrase 'that hidden time/age' with نهان postpositive. So: 'that hidden age/time became exposed.'
segments.s080-g87: به رستم is the indirect object 'to/for Rostam' (به = 'to/for'). همی is an emphatic/temporal particle here (narrative marker). داد is the past tense 'gave'. ده is the numeral 'ten' modifying دایه شیر 'wet‑nurses' (دایه = nurse, شیر = 'milk' when describing a wet nurse — 'دایهٔ شیر' = 'a wet nurse'). The whole means 'they gave Rostam ten wet nurses.'
segments.s080-g88: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'because/for'. نیروی is a construct/genitive 'the strength/power of...' and مردست here is a poetic contraction of مرد است ('is manly' or 'is of a man'), yielding 'his strength is manly' or 'he has a man's strength.' و = 'and'. سرمایه is used in the sense 'source/stock/lineage' and شیر here is 'lion' (i.e. 'his stock is of lions' — a poetic way of saying his lineage or source is leonine). The clause gives the reason for the wet nurses: his manly strength and leonine stock.
segments.s080-g49: پس is an adverb meaning 'then' placed at the start to link actions. آن پیکر is a demonstrative noun phrase 'that body' (آن = 'that', پیکر = 'body'); رستم follows as a name in apposition/genitive (classical/poetic word order often omits the ezāfe). شیرخوار is a postpositive epithet 'the suckling/infant', describing Rostam.
segments.s080-g50: ببردند is the 3rd person plural past 'they carried/took' (classical form with ب-). نزدیک functions as an adverb/prepositional word 'near/close to' governing the following NP. سام سوار is a name plus epithet: 'Sam, the horseman/rider' (sوار postposed to سام).
segments.s080-g51: ابر here is used metaphorically ('a cloud') to describe hair en masse; the phrase reads as 'a cloud of Sam the hero's hair' with the possessive relation implied by word order (classical poetry often omits the ezāfe). موی بر پای خاست is the verb phrase 'the hair rose up to (or reached) his feet' (خاست = 'arose').
segments.s080-g52: مرا ماند is an idiomatic verbless/auxiliary construction meaning roughly 'it occurred to me' or 'to me remained/it came to mind' (مرا = 'to me', ماند = 'remained/occurred'). این پرنیان is the noun phrase 'this silken robe/garment'. گفت here would normally be the past 'he said', and راست is an evaluative adverb/adjective 'true/proper/fit'. Note: the printed senses list does not include the simple past sense of گفت, so the mapping below marks that mismatch.
segments.s080-g53: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). نیم ازین پیکر is the subject phrase 'half of this body' (از + این: 'of this'); آید is the verb 'comes/should come' (potential/optative present used in condition). تنش (تن + -ش) = 'his body/frame' — the clause means 'if half of this body should come upon his frame.'
segments.s080-g54: سرش = 'his head' (سر + enclitic -ش). ابر here is 'the clouds' used in the expression 'reach the clouds'; ساید functions as a verbal/adjectival predicate meaning 'will reach/touch' (poetic/archaic use). زمین دامنش is a parallel phrase: 'the earth (will be) his hem' i.e. 'his hem will sweep the ground' (دامنش = 'his hem/skirts', again with enclitic -ش).
segments.s080-g55: وزان پس is a connective adverbial 'thereupon/after that'; فرستاده را is the definite object 'the messenger' (را marks definiteness/object). پیش خواست is a compound verb phrase 'called before/forward' — خواست is past 'he desired/asked' used here as 'he summoned/called.'
segments.s080-g56: درم ریخت is literal 'he poured out dirhams/coins' (درم = coin; ریخت here 'poured/scattered (coins)'). تا introduces a result/purpose 'so that/until'; بر سرش گشت راست is a result clause 'it became right/upright/proper upon his head' — گشت = 'became/turned', راست = 'right/proper' (i.e. he was made properly adorned or set up).
segments.s080-g65: نخست is an adverb meaning 'first/initially' placed before the verb. آفرین here is a nominal/verb phrase 'praise' (as in 'to praise'), and کرد is the light verb ('did/made') forming the verbal phrase آفرین کرد = 'praised'. بر + کردگار is a prepositional phrase 'toward/for the Creator' (بر here = 'to/toward'), with کردگار as the object.
segments.s080-g66: This is a verbless nominal clause typical of poetic Persian: بران ('on/against/on that one' referring back to کردگار) + the noun phrase شادمان گردش روزگار functions as a predicate meaning 'the joyous course/turning of the world.' The finite copula 'was/fell' is omitted in poetry and understood from context.
segments.s080-g67: ستودن is the infinitive/masdar 'to praise'. گرفت here is used with an infinitive to form a periphrastic past 'began to …' (گرفت + infinitive = 'began to do'), and آنگهی ('then/thereupon') sets the temporal frame. زال را is the definite object/recipient (را marks it).
segments.s080-g68: This noun phrase (خداوند شمشیر و کوپال را) lacks an explicit verb in the line — a common poetic elision — but syntactically reads as 'the lord (خداوند) [gave/presented] the sword and Kupāl (شمشیر و کوپال) (را marks the definite objects)'. Here خداوند functions as 'the lord/sovereign' rather than the theological 'God.'
segments.s080-g69: پس آمد = 'then came' (پس setting sequence, آمد past). بدان is a fused locative/directional 'به + آن' = 'to/unto that'; in the source the parts are split but together mean 'to that'. پیکر پرنیان is the noun 'figure/body' qualified by پرنیان 'silken-clad' (an adjectival/epithetic phrase).
segments.s080-g70: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. یال یلان داشت is a compact predicate: یال 'mane/crest' + یلان (plural of یل, 'heroes') = 'the mane/glory of heroes', and داشت is simple past 'had'. و joins the next phrase فر کیان = 'the royal glory/splendour of kings' (فر = royal glory, کیان = kings).
segments.s080-g71: بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb meaning 'he said/commanded'. کین را uses را to mark کین as the direct object (کین here = 'this vengeance/that enmity' or a named object to be preserved). چنین ارجمند is a predicative adjective phrase 'so/thus esteemed/precious' modifying کین.
segments.s080-g72: بدارید is the polite/imperative form (2nd person plural) of داشتن used here as 'keep/maintain' (an order). کز = contraction of که از 'from which' here meaning 'from which / so that from (even) a ...'. دم = 'breath/moment' (an idiom meaning 'even a moment'). نیابد is a subjunctive/optative third-person form ('may not find/receive'); in this idiom نیابد گزند = 'suffer no harm', with گزند = 'harm'.
segments.s080-g25: یکی کودکی دوختند از حریر is SOV with an omitted plural subject ('they'). یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' modifying کودکی (a child), دوختند is 3rd-person plural past 'sewed/made', and از حریر is a postpositional phrase 'out of/from silk' specifying the material.
segments.s080-g26: به بالای آن is a locative prepositional phrase 'on top of that'; شیر ناخورده شیر is a short noun phrase where ناخورده (a past-participial adjective 'not-having-drunk / unweaned') modifies شیر, and the final شیر repeats/emphatically names the animal (poetic apposition) — overall 'an unweaned lion'.
segments.s080-g27: درون وی is a locative phrase 'inside it/its interior' (وی = it/its), آگنده is a predicative adjective 'filled' with an implied copula, and موی سمور is a genitival phrase 'hair/fur of the sable' specifying what fills it.
segments.s080-g28: به رخ بر is a doubled locative 'on the face' (poetic overlap of به and بر), نگاریده is a past participle 'painted/adorned' used predicatively, and ناهید و هور are the names/personifications presented as what is depicted on the face.
segments.s080-g29: به بازوش is 'on his arm' with the possessive suffix -ش, بر is an additional locative 'on/upon', and اژدهای دلیر is the noun 'a brave dragon' (اژدهای = dragon, دلیر = brave) describing what is on the arm.
segments.s080-g30: به چنگ اندرش داده is a passive/result phrase 'given into his grip' (داده = given, اندرش = 'in/into it/his'), where چنگ functions as 'grasp' or 'hold'; چنگال شیر is a genitive 'the lion's talon/claw' — overall 'they put the lion's talon into his grasp.'
segments.s080-g31: به زیر is 'beneath / under', کش (from کشیدن 'to draw') and گرفته (past participle 'taken/drawn') form a verbal cluster meaning 'drawn/drawn up', اندر is locative 'inside/in', and سنان is the object 'the spear (Sanan)' — together implying 'he had drawn the spear Sanan under/inside (his garment).' The copula is implied in poetic diction.
segments.s080-g32: به یک دست is 'in one hand', کوپال names the weapon/item held in that hand, و دیگر عنان continues with 'and in the other (hand) the reins' — دست is omitted the second time and implied; عنان = reins/bridle.
segments.s080-g89: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از شیر آمد is a simple past clause: از + شیر ('from the milk/breast') + آمد (he came/was weaned). سوی خوردنی is a directional/goal phrase 'toward eating/food' (سوی = toward, خوردنی here as a noun 'food/solid food'). The whole segment means 'when he came from the milk toward eating.'
segments.s080-g90: شد is the past of شدن 'became' (here introducing the result). از نان و از گوشت is an ablative/agent-like phrase 'from/by bread and by meat' (i.e. his diet was supplemented with bread and meat). افزودنی is used adjectivally/participially ('added/augmenting' or 'additional'), describing what was given to him.
segments.s080-g91: This line contains archaic/obscure items. بدی appears as a temporal/sequential marker (likely a contracted/poetic form related to 'بعد' = 'after' but is not the common modern sense 'bad'); پنج مرده is an unclear measure phrase (probably 'five times/periods' or an archaic age-measure); مراو is an old/obscure pronoun/word (listed as obscure in the lexical data). را is the definite object marker, and خورش is the noun 'stew/cooked dish' — so the segment says roughly 'after five [units] his food (stew)...' (the exact values of بدی/پنج مرده/مراو are archaic/uncertain).
segments.s080-g92: بماندند is the past plural of ماندن 'they remained/continued' (here meaning 'they continued' or 'were left to do something'). مردم is the subject 'the people', placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in poetry). ازان is the ablative 'from that', and پرورش is the noun 'upbringing/nurture'. The clause thus means 'the people continued his upbringing from that.'
segments.s080-g93: چو is again the temporal 'when'. رستم is the subject name. بپیمود is a past/telic verb from پیمودن 'to traverse/complete/reach' and here means 'reached' or 'attained'. بالای (followed by a numeral) functions as 'at the height/age of' — so بالای هشت = 'at/around eight'. The clause reads 'when Rostam reached eight.'
segments.s080-g94: بسان is the simile marker 'like/as'. یکی serves as an indefinite/numberal 'one/a single'. سرو is the metaphoric noun 'cypress' (a standard Persian image for a tall, graceful person). آزاد here is an epithet meaning 'lofty/noble' (in poetry often 'upright/lofty' rather than strictly 'free'), and گشت is past of گشتن 'became'. The pattern 'بسان ... گشت' = 'he became like ...'.
segments.s080-g95: چنان شد 'it became thus/so' introduces a result clause; که is the subordinating 'that' linking to the result-clause. رخشان is an adjective 'radiant/bright' modifying ستاره 'star'. شود is the non-past/subjunctive form of شدن 'become' used here to state the resulting condition ('becomes/is made into'). The clause means 'it became such that he became a radiant star.'
segments.s080-g96: جهان is 'the world/people' (the observing public). بر is a preposition 'upon/at/toward' governing ستاره 'the star' (the referent just described). نظاره is a noun 'gazing/observation' and شود (again) marks the resulting state ('becomes/shall be observed' — narrative optative/impersonal). Together: 'the world will/shall gaze upon the star' (i.e. people will look to him as to a star).
segments.s080-g73: The phrase places the noun 'نیایش' (prayer) first, then the habitual/progressive particle 'همی' before the past-tense verb 'کردم' (I did/made) to express a repeated or continuous past action: 'I kept praying.' 'اندر نهان' is a prepositional phrase ('in/within secret') where 'نهان' functions adverbially ('in secret'). Word order (object/nominal phrase → aspect particle → verb → locative) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s080-g74: 'شب و روز' is a fixed adverbial pair meaning 'day and night.' The preposition 'با' introduces the addressee/companion ('with' / here: indicating the deity addressed), followed by the compound noun 'کردگار جهان' = 'the Creator of the world' (noun + modifier). In prayer contexts 'با' has the sense of addressing or being with/unto God.
segments.s080-g75: The clause begins with the complementizer 'که' ('that'), introducing a subordinate clause of purpose or result. 'زنده' ('alive') is an adverbial/ predicative modifier to the action of 'ببیند' — here 'ببیند' is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive (optative/potential) of 'دیدن' used to express hope or purpose: 'may see alive.' 'جهانبین من' is a postposed possessive phrase 'my world-seer' (noun + possessive 'من'). Word order places the verb before its noun phrase in a poetic inverted order.
segments.s080-g76: 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' (from). 'تخم تو' = 'your seed/offspring' (noun + postposed pronoun). 'گردی' in this line functions as a verbal form ('you become' / 'you shall be made') — an archaic/poetic conjugation of گردیدن/شدن — even though the token list gives only nominal senses; thus its verbal force should be read here. 'به آیین من' marks result/state ('into my way/faith') with 'به' meaning 'into/toward' and 'آیین' meaning way/ritual/practice; the final 'من' is the possessor 'my.'
segments.s080-g77: 'کنون شد' = 'now there became / now has come to pass' — 'شد' is the past/coprial verb 'became/occurred.' The sequence 'مرا و ترا' is the older cliticized form meaning 'for me and for you' (each originally 'مرا' and 'ترا'); here the clitics are split in the tokenization but function as indirect objects. 'پشت راست' is a nominal predicate meaning 'a right/true support' (پشت = support/protection, راست = true/right/firm). The overall construction is existential/coprular: 'Now there became for me and you a firm support.'
segments.s080-g78: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal meaning 'one ought not / one must not / it is not proper.' The structure 'نباید ... خواست' uses the past-form verb 'خواست' nominally as 'to ask/request' (elliptical infinitival sense): 'one should not request ...' 'جز از' sets up the sole exception ('except from / except for'), and 'زندگانیش' (زندگان + ـش) here means 'his being alive' or 'his living' (the state of being alive). So the clause says essentially 'one should ask for nothing except his life.'
segments.s080-g79: 'فرستاده آمد' is subject + past verb 'a messenger came.' 'چو' introduces a simile ('like/as'), and 'باد دمان' is a poetic compound literally 'wind of the hem/skirt' — an image for a light, swift gust. The simile modifies the manner of arrival: 'came like a gust at the hem' (i.e., very quickly).
segments.s080-g80: 'بر' here marks the target/toward whom the messenger came ('to/upon Zāl'). 'زال' is the proper name. The adjectives 'روشن‌دل و شادمان' follow the noun, joined by 'و', and qualify Zāl as 'clear-/bright‑hearted and joyful.' This is a common adjective ordering (noun → adjective(s)) in classical poetry.
segments.s080-g57: به شادی is an adverbial phrase of manner ('with joy / joyfully') where به + noun = adverb (به = sense 'by/-ly'). برآمد is a third‑person past/punctual verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز introduces the source ('from') and links to the following noun phrase درگاه کوس ('the court/threshold of the drum' — درگاه here = 'royal court'). The whole segment reads literally 'with joy issued forth from the court the drum.'
segments.s080-g58: بیاراست is a past verb 'adorned' placed before its object (a common poetic order). میدان is the place being adorned ('arena/field'). چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), followed by چشم خروس ('the rooster's eye') as the point of comparison. The sense is: 'he adorned the field, like the eye of a rooster.'
segments.s080-g59: می‌آورد is the narrative imperfect/present‑habitual form of آورد ('he brought/placed' or 'he caused to come'). و simply coordinates the clauses. رامشگران را is the definite object (را marks the object) — here 'the entertainers/comforters' — and بخواند is a third‑person past/epic narrative form meaning 'he called/summoned' (in classical epic prose/poetry the b‑prefixed form appears in narrative). Together: 'he brought and summoned the entertainers.'
segments.s080-g60: به marks the beneficiary/recipient ('to/for the'), خواهندگان is 'the seekers/petitioners'. بر before درم functions as the prepositional element taking the thing scattered ('upon/with dirhams'), and برفشاند is the past third‑person verb 'he scattered/strewn (upon)'. The whole clause means 'he showered dirhams upon the petitioners.'
segments.s080-g61: بیاراست is the past verb 'he adorned/made splendid' and takes جشنی ('a feast/celebration') as its object. که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that/which'), whose subject begins خورشید و ماه ('the sun and the moon'). The segment sets up 'a feast (that) the sun and moon ...'.
segments.s080-g62: نظاره شده(اند) is a compound predication where نظاره (a noun meaning 'gaze/observation') combines with the auxiliary شدند ('they became / they were') to mean 'they looked on / were spectators'. اندرَان is a locative ('therein / in that place') and بزمگاه names the place ('banquet‑hall'). Word order is typical poetic: verb + locative + place = 'they beheld therein the banquet‑hall.'
segments.s080-g63: پس is a temporal/discourse adverb ('then'). آن نامه ('that letter') is topicalized and refers to the letter just mentioned; زال is the subject (Zal). پاسخ نوشت is a light verb phrase 'wrote a reply' (پاسخ = reply, نوشت = wrote). The clause literally: 'Then, regarding that letter, Zal wrote a reply.'
segments.s080-g64: بیاراست here qualifies how the reply was composed — 'adorned / made beautiful' — and is followed by a simile introduced by چون ('like'), with مرغزار بهشت ('a meadow of paradise') as the image. Grammatically this postpositive phrase describes the reply (i.e., 'he wrote a reply, adorned like a heavenly meadow').

sentences.s080-s49: You said that Sam was heroic with regard to rank, high station, renown, learning, and counsel.
sentences.s080-s5: Men and women were left astonished at this, for no one had heard of a child with the body of an elephant.
sentences.s080-s6: They stitched up that very sore place, and they entrusted all the pain to the medicine.
sentences.s080-s7: Day and night the mother had been in wine-induced sleep; from that sleep, alas, her wits had gone.
sentences.s080-s8: When the youth awoke from sleep, he opened his lips to Sindukht and began to speak.
sentences.s080-s21: They held a celebration in the rose-garden, from Zavolestan as far as Kabulestan.
sentences.s080-s22: The whole plain was full of wine and ney (reed-flutes); in every nook there were a hundred festal assemblies.
sentences.s080-s23: Throughout Zavolestan, from edge to edge, sitters (entertainers/comforters) were seated in every place.
sentences.s080-s24: There was no one inferior to the elders sitting below; they sat together like warp and weft.
sentences.s080-s1: A certain oily‑handed mobad came, and he made that moon‑faced beauty drunk for me.
sentences.s080-s2: Let it be arranged, without trouble, at the moon's side; turn the child's head off the road for me.
sentences.s080-s3: Bring him out so unharmed that no one in the world has seen such a marvel.
sentences.s080-s4: Take up a certain sickly child, like a round milk‑white orb; lift him high and bring him into view.
sentences.s080-s17: They then seated him upon the war-steed, and a few attendants also gathered around him.
sentences.s080-s18: When the affair was entirely finished and all was made ready, they handled it as it ought to be.
sentences.s080-s19: They roused the fierce raiders, and at that command they poured out dirhams.
sentences.s080-s20: Then they brought that likeness of Rostam, mace-bearing, close to Sam the rider.
sentences.s080-s9: They showered him with gold and jewels; the Creator's cloud cried "Afarin" (bravo).
sentences.s080-s10: They hurried that child before him and exalted him like something heavenly.
sentences.s080-s11: He laughed at that child — the dark-cypress — and saw in him the royal radiance.
sentences.s080-s12: Rostam said, 'Grief has come upon my head.' They named the boy Rostam.
sentences.s080-s41: When Zal heard those pithy words — which brightened the spirit and entered the mind.
sentences.s080-s42: His joy increased into exultation; he lifted his neck toward the azure sky.
sentences.s080-s43: He wandered for a while across the face of the world; that hidden time became bare (was revealed).
sentences.s080-s44: They gave Rostam ten wet‑nurses (nurses for milk), for his strength is manly and his stock is of lions.
sentences.s080-s25: Then they carried that infant body of Rostam close to Sam the horseman.
sentences.s080-s26: A cloud of Sam the hero's hair rose to his feet; it occurred to me — 'this silken robe,' he said, 'is true/proper.'
sentences.s080-s27: If even half of this body's bulk should come to his frame, his head will reach the clouds and his hem will sweep the earth.
sentences.s080-s28: Thereupon he summoned the messenger forward and poured out dirhams until, upon his head, it became right (i.e. he was properly adorned).
sentences.s080-s33: First he praised the Creator; upon him fell the joyous turning of the world.
sentences.s080-s34: Then he began to praise Zāl; the lord gave him the sword and Kupāl.
sentences.s080-s35: Then he approached that silken-clad figure who bore the mane of heroes and the royal splendour of kings.
sentences.s080-s36: He commanded, 'Keep this kīn so treasured that it suffer no harm even from a breath.'
sentences.s080-s13: They sewed a child out of silk; on top of it an unweaned lion.
sentences.s080-s14: Its interior was filled with sable fur; on its face Nahid and Hoor were painted.
sentences.s080-s15: On his arm (was) a brave dragon; into his grasp they placed the lion's talon.
sentences.s080-s16: He had drawn the spear Sanan beneath (his clothes); in one hand (he held) Kupāl and in the other the reins.
sentences.s080-s45: When he left the breast and turned to eating, bread and meat were added to him.
sentences.s080-s46: For five (periods) his food was a stew; the people continued his upbringing from that.
sentences.s080-s47: When Rostam reached (the age of) eight, he became like a single lofty cypress.
sentences.s080-s48: He became such that he shone like a radiant star; the world would gaze upon that star.
sentences.s080-s37: I continually prayed in secret, day and night, with the Creator of the world.
sentences.s080-s38: That my world-seer may see him alive, and that from your seed you may be brought into my way (my faith).
sentences.s080-s39: Now there has come to me and to you a true (or firm) support; one should ask for nothing except his being alive.
sentences.s080-s40: A messenger came like a gust to the hem (i.e., swiftly) to Zāl, who was bright‑hearted and joyful.
sentences.s080-s29: A drum sounded joyfully from the court, and the square was arrayed like a rooster's eye.
sentences.s080-s30: He brought (them) and summoned the entertainers; he showered dirhams upon the petitioners.
sentences.s080-s31: He made a feast so splendid that the sun and the moon were spectators in that banquet‑hall.
sentences.s080-s32: Then Zal wrote the reply to that letter, adorning it like a meadow of paradise.
